summary: My name is Paul Nirens, and I live in Israel. I come from a mixed Jewish family with roots in France, Poland, England, and Australia. I grew up around food since my father owned a steak restaurant in Melbourne, and I became a chef myself before transitioning into a career focused on Galilean cuisine here in Israel. I immigrated to Israel in 1984 after several visits and have been deeply involved in the local food scene ever since.

I was interviewed at the Efendi Hotel in Akko in July 2023 for the Food & Foodways in Israel Palestine Project. Akko is where I shared my story about food and my journey from Australia to Israel. It’s a place that connects to my current life and work in Israel’s food culture.
cues: Food & Foodways in Israel Palestine Project

entity: Louis B. Nunn Center for Oral History
summary: The oral history interview I participated in at Akko will be housed at the Louis B. Nunn Center for Oral History in Lexington, Kentucky. This center is preserving my story as part of their Food & Foodways in Israel Palestine Project. It’s an honor to have my experiences documented there.
cues: Food & Foodways in Israel Palestine Project

summary: My father was born in France, which was part of our family’s journey. My grandfather left Poland for France because he thought it was more enlightened for Jews, but before World War II, he realized France wasn’t as safe as he hoped. This led the family to move again, eventually to Australia.

My grandfather was originally from Poland and was a man of vision who left Eastern Europe because he felt it wasn’t a good place for Jews. He was part of the Polish Jewish community, and I remember my grandfather attending the Warsaw Landsmanshaftn in Melbourne. Poland is a key part of my family’s heritage.

Australia became home to my family after leaving Europe, as my grandfather wanted to get as far away as possible from Europe before World War II. My mother’s side also came to Australia, though as convicts from England in the 1830s. I grew up in Melbourne, where my family owned a steak restaurant, and I lived there until I made aliyah in 1984.

On my mother’s side, we discovered that our ancestors came from England as convicts in the 1830s. This was a surprising part of my family history, contrasting with my father’s side. England is part of my heritage through my maternal lineage.

Melbourne is where I grew up and where my family owned a steak restaurant called the Barbecue Inn. It was a vibrant Jewish community with strong ties to Polish Jewish culture. I also attended Mount Scopus Memorial College there, the largest Jewish day school in the diaspora.

My father’s steak restaurant in Melbourne operated before Wagyu beef became popular, so we served quality Australian beef instead. Wagyu wasn’t part of our menu back then, but we focused on good barbecue and steak dishes. It was a different era in the steak business.

At my father’s restaurant in Melbourne, we had a Yugoslavian pastry and cold portion chef before Yugoslavia broke apart. She taught us many Central European dishes like cevapcici, which influenced our menu and my food knowledge. This exposure helped shape my appreciation for diverse cuisines.

Cevapcici was one of the traditional Balkan dishes we learned to make at my father’s restaurant in Melbourne. It’s a garlic-filled kabob from the Balkans, and our Yugoslavian chef made her own sausages. This dish was part of the diverse culinary influences I grew up with.

My father used to hang a duck up to dry it out to make Peking duck at home, showing his adventurous approach to food. This was in the 1970s in Melbourne, and it was part of the diverse cooking techniques I was exposed to growing up. It inspired my own food exploration.

My father also made wontons from scratch, which was part of his experimental and inquisitive approach to food. Growing up with such diverse culinary experiences, including making wontons, influenced my passion for food and cooking. It was all part of my food adventurer identity.

I immigrated to Israel in 1984 after several visits starting in 1978 for gap year programs. I lived on a kibbutz in the Western Galilee and eventually settled in the community neighborhood of Tuval. Israel is where I built my family, career, and deepened my connection to Jewish identity and Galilean cuisine.

On my way from Australia to Israel in 1984, I traveled overland and flew to Bali as part of my journey. From Bali, I continued overland through Asia, aiming to reach Israel while experiencing different cultures and foods. Bali was a key stop on this adventurous route.

During my 1984 journey to Israel, I traveled across land through India. I wanted to continue into Pakistan and Afghanistan but found it too dangerous at the time. India was part of this formative travel experience that combined culture and food exploration.

I had planned to travel through Pakistan on my way to Israel in 1984, but it was too dangerous then, so I had to change my route. This was part of my overland journey from Australia through Asia, which was both adventurous and challenging. Pakistan was a place I wanted to explore but couldn’t at that time.

Afghanistan was another country I intended to cross during my 1984 journey to Israel, but due to safety concerns, I avoided it. This was during a turbulent time in the region, so I flew to Italy instead. Afghanistan was part of the broader context of my adventurous travel route.

After traveling overland through Asia and avoiding dangerous areas, I flew into Italy in 1984 as part of my journey to Israel. Italy was a key waypoint before completing my aliyah. It marked the transition from my long overland trip to settling in Israel.

I was involved in the Jewish youth movement Habonim during my early visits to Israel starting in 1978. Habonim was part of my connection to Jewish identity and community, helping me build ties before making aliyah. It was my first youth group before I joined Hashomer Hatzair.

After Habonim, I joined Hashomer Hatzair, a more left-wing Jewish youth movement, during my time in Israel. It was through this group that I met Ilya, who became my wife. Hashomer Hatzair played an important role in my social and ideological life in Israel.

While my parents weren’t overtly Zionist, I had a strong Jewish identity and was involved in Zionist youth movements like Habonim and Hashomer Hatzair. I felt a Zionist fervor when I made aliyah in 1984, believing Israel was where I belonged. Zionism influenced my decision to stay and build a life here.

I attended Mount Scopus Memorial College in Melbourne, which is the largest Jewish day school in the diaspora. It was a significant part of my upbringing and Jewish education. The school connected me to Jewish culture and community before I moved to Israel.

My grandfather was connected to the Warsaw Landsmanshaftn, a Polish Jewish community organization that still existed in Melbourne in the 1970s. This link to Warsaw was part of my family’s heritage and the preservation of Polish Jewish culture abroad. It was a strong community connection for us.

In Israel, we lived in the community neighborhood of Tuval in the Western Galilee mountains. We were once members of the kibbutz there but later moved to the community area. Tuval has been home for raising our four children and building our life in Israel.

I lived on a kibbutz in the mountains of the Western Galilee when my children were young, and later in the community neighborhood of Tuval nearby. The Western Galilee is where I have focused much of my culinary work, specializing in Galilean cuisine that includes local Arabic influences. It’s a region I deeply know and love.
